name: Alpine Central Europe (Switzerland / Austria, ~1200m)

staple_foods.name: alpine butter (Alpbutter/Bergbutter)

primary_metabolites.lipid_content: 82-84 g/100g [alpine grass-fed butter — Leiber et al. 2005 J Dairy Sci 88:1316]
primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 21-25% of total fatty acids [alpine grass-fed butter — Leiber et al. 2005; higher than lowland butter]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 2.0-2.8% of total fatty acids [alpine butter — Leiber et al. 2005]
primary_metabolites.palmitic_acid: 24-27% of total fatty acids [alpine butter — Leiber et al. 2005]
primary_metabolites.stearic_acid: 10-13% of total fatty acids [alpine butter — Leiber et al. 2005]

key_flavor_bioactives.conjugated_linoleic_acid: 3.0-5.5 g/100g fat [alpine grass-fed butter CLA — Leiber et al. 2005 J Dairy Sci 88:1316; 3-4x higher than lowland grain-fed butter; Alpine herb-rich pasture CLA signal]
key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 35-60 mg/kg [alpine butter — Leiber et al. 2005; higher alpha-tocopherol vs lowland butter from carotenoid-rich alpine grasses]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 10-25 mg GAE/100g [butter polyphenols — minor but measurable in grass-fed alpine butter; Collomb et al. 2002]

staple_foods.cultivar: Bos taurus (Brown Swiss / Simmental, alpine grass-fed)
staple_foods.notes: Alpine grass-fed butter from summer alpine pasture (Alp) — the flagship fat of Swiss and Austrian mountain food culture. The high CLA content (3-5.5 g/100g fat) is 3-4x higher than lowland dairy butter and is the defining terroir fat biomarker. Alpine pastures with diverse herbs (clover, plantain, chicory, alpine flowers) at elevation transfer specific fatty acids and fat-soluble vitamins to bovine milk fat. Leiber et al. (2005) is the canonical reference demonstrating the altitude/forage diet signal in dairy fat. Natural experiment group: RSU-36 (alpine, ~1200m), RSU-37 (Irish Atlantic, ~100m), RSU-43 (Nordic lowland, ~30m) — same fat category (bovine butter), contrasting altitude/forage environments.
data_sources: Leiber et al. (2005) — Effect of high altitude grazing on milk composition and fatty acid patterns, J Dairy Sci 88:1316
Collomb et al. (2002) — Conjugated linoleic acid and trans fatty acids in alpine and lowland milk, J Dairy Sci 85:1239

staple_foods.name: rye bread (Walliser Roggenbrot)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 300-600 mg GAE/100g [rye bread — ferulic acid dominant; Andersson et al. 2010 Food Chem 119:240]

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.5-1.2 g/100g [sourdough rye bread — strong LAB acidification; Arendt et al. 2007]
organic_acids.acetic_acid: 0.2-0.5 g/100g [sourdough rye — acetic acid from heterofermentative LAB]

staple_foods.notes: Walliser Roggenbrot is a traditional Swiss Alpine sourdough rye bread with IGP status — among the densest, most intensely fermented breads in the European tradition. The high-altitude Valais rye has historically been the survival carbohydrate of alpine communities. Strong LAB sourdough fermentation produces a complex organic acid profile.
data_sources: Andersson et al. (2010) — Phenolic antioxidants in rye bread, Food Chem 119:240

staple_foods.name: alpine cheese (Gruyère / Appenzeller)

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.3-0.6 g/100g [aged alpine cheese — partially consumed during aging]
organic_acids.propionic_acid: 0.3-0.8 g/100g [Gruyère — Propionibacterium fermentation produces characteristic nutty notes; Thierry et al. 2002 Lait 82:31]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.8-1.5 g/100g free glutamate [Gruyère 10-12 month aged — Weimer et al. 2016]

staple_foods.cultivar: Bos taurus (Brown Swiss, alpine milk — Gruyère AOP)
staple_foods.notes: Gruyère AOP — Swiss hard alpine cheese, 10-12 month aging on mountain pasture milk. The Propionibacterium freudenreichii fermentation during aging produces propionic acid (characteristic nutty/sweet flavor) and characteristic eyes. The alpine milk — from grass-fed cows at elevation — has elevated CLA and tocopherol in the fat fraction that is partially retained in the cheese. Free glutamate from proteolysis is significant after 10+ months aging.
data_sources: Thierry et al. (2002) — Propionic acid bacteria in Swiss cheeses, Lait 82:31

staple_foods.name: sauerkraut (fermented white cabbage)

key_flavor_bioactives.isothiocyanates: trace [sauerkraut — glucosinolates partially hydrolyzed during fermentation; degraded by LAB; Ciska et al. 2009]

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 1.0-2.0 g/100g [sauerkraut — dominant LAB product; Joshi et al. 2016]

staple_foods.cultivar: Brassica oleracea (white cabbage, traditional Alsace/Swiss type)
staple_foods.notes: Sauerkraut — the central fermented food of the Alpine and Germanic tradition. LAB fermentation (Leuconostoc, Lactobacillus) of shredded white cabbage produces a highly acidified, preserved product. Strong lactic acid signal. Historically critical for vitamin C provision during Alpine winters (preserved ascorbic acid).
data_sources: Joshi et al. (2016) — Fermentation of vegetables, Food Biotechnol 30:1

staple_foods.name: wild alpine herbs (Kräuter: yarrow, thyme, gentian)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 4000-8000 mg GAE/100g [dried alpine herbs — thyme, yarrow, gentian; Zitterl-Eglseer et al. 2015 Nat Prod Commun 10:1697]

terpenes.linalool: present — thyme chemotype linalool present in some strains
terpenes.pinene: present — alpha-pinene in Artemisia and related alpine herbs
terpenes.myrcene: present — thyme essential oil secondary terpene

staple_foods.notes: Alpine wild herb complex — the aromatic signature of high-altitude Alpine food culture. Includes thyme, yarrow, gentian (bitters), alpine clover, and various Apiaceae. The high UV at altitude drives elevated secondary metabolite production. Alpine herb teas, herb schnapps (Kräuterlikör), and fresh herb use in alpine cheese production are the primary applications. The polyphenol and terpene profiles from altitude-stressed plants at 1200m are significantly higher than lowland counterparts.
data_sources: Zitterl-Eglseer et al. (2015) — Antioxidant properties of alpine medicinal plants, Nat Prod Commun 10:1697

known_processing_methods: alpine cheese production (thermophilic LAB + Propionibacterium aging)

notes: Swiss/Austrian Alpine zone (~1200m), metamorphic basement geology, cold-cool climate (mean 6.5°C), high precipitation. The central fat signal here is CLA in alpine butter — arguably the cleanest altitude-forage-terroir signal in the entire dataset. The Leiber et al. (2005) study directly demonstrates that altitude and alpine herb-rich pasture drive CLA elevation. This RSU anchors the alpine end of the butter natural experiment group (RSU-36 alpine, RSU-37 Irish Atlantic, RSU-43 Nordic lowland). The cheese-rye-sauerkraut fermentation system is one of the most fermentation-dense food cultures in the dataset.
